b CURRENT AFFAIRS.

- The depreciation in the market price of the “ Simply
Deplorable’s ”’ shares did not prevent the staff of the
Company that runs that organ of unenlightened and

- chicken-hearted freedom from eating an annual dinner
“on Saturday last. Strange to say, the first toast was
not “the XKing.”” Though the “Deplorable’’ pub-
lishes some Irish every day, there was not as much as
one song in Inish on the musical programme. We are
_ told in the report that someone sang ‘ The Moon hath
raised ”’; well, that is more than the “Freeman’s’’

" shares have dome lately!

. The speech of the gentleman who proposed “ Ireland

" a Nation ”’ at the festive “ Deplorable’’ gathering, at
which not even one song.in Irish was sung, was a very
creditable rendering of 7aimess. The orator dzclared : —
“ They had heard much of the Anglicising of Treland,
“he believed there never was a time when they
Jess in danger of being absorbed than they were
resent, never a time when their people appreciated

5

so well their own genius and destiny, and insisted so
practically upon their -distinct Natioaality.”” ~ And
this at a gtahering at which there was not even one
song in Irish!

Mr. J. P. Gaynor, in responding, said that ¢ The
motto of the ‘Freeman’s Journal’ was ‘Ireland a
Nation.””” We should rather have suspected that its
sad motto at the present time was “ Stin Leac eleven
per cent.””! Mr. Gaynor also said:—*The cause of
Ireland a Nation and the ‘Freeman’s Journal’ had
been indissoluble for many a long year in this country.’’
If so, it was no wonder that Ireland a Nation was
going down like the Freeman’s shares!

Mr. Brayden, the editor, proposed “Success to the
‘ Freeman’s Journal,” Ltd.”” The “Deplorable’’ does
not report ‘his speech! Under all the circumstances,
the editor of the “Freeman’’ had a difficult task to
perform. The gentleman who responded gave another
dexterious exhibition in the art of raimeis. He said:—
“It ds the great National organ of the country, without
peer or without rival, speaking with authority for the
nation, voicing the feelings and sentiments of every
Nationalist, lending its powerful aid and influence to_
every movement for the welfare and happiness of the
people (applause).”” No one of the audience exclaimed
either “Sapho’ or “Kitty Grey’’! Amongst the
other things that, according to the orator, the “De-
plorable ’’ stands for is “the obliterating of religious
animosity, prejudice and class feeling.”” ~Certainly it
has not made many enemies in the cause of Tolerance,
Justice and Fair Play. In so far as what the orator
styled “religious animosity ”’ can be obliterated by
being “tamo’’ before the Ascendancy, the ¢ Deplor-
able ’’ has done a considerable share in the  obliterat-
ing’’ line. Mr. Sexton, apparently, showed his appre-
ciation of the occasion by keeping away from it.

We have all heard of the boy who whistles in order
to keep up his courage as he passes a grave-yard. In
these days of depreciating values in Freeman’
ordinary shares, it was, no doubt, to be expected‘ th?,t
the staff would break out into sun-bursting raimers.
Unfortunately, the Stock Exchange refuses to be led
by raimess! 2

The Munster Feis of this year ought to be a popular
success, as well as a success from other points of view.
A meeting of supporters of the Feis was held recently
in the City Hall. Father O’Brien, C.C., is Hon. Sec.
of the Feis, and the meeting in the City Hall was pre-
sided over by Father E. Fitzgerald, C.C. There was
some vigorous speaking. A large number of influential
people were co-opted on the General Council of the Feis,
including the Lord Mayor of Cork; Dr. ‘Windle, Presi-
dent Q.C.C.; Augustine Roche, M.P'.;_ Professor
Stockley, M. J, Horgan, solr.; T. B. Lillis, Munster
Bank; J. F. Delaney, City Engineer; J. M. Hogan,
Hibernian Bank; Rev. M. Murphy, Rev. J. Cahalgn,
Rev. Bro. Aloysius, Superior-General Presentation
Order; Rev. Bro, Walker, North Monastery. Thgﬁe
names, to go no further, indicate that the Munster Feis
of 1907 has secured very strong backing. Mr. Roche,
M.P., promised a special prize, Dr. Windle promised
another, and Mr. John O’Connell another.

Weo have received the first number of “Hermes,” a
new illustrated University literary quarterly, which is
published from University College, Stephen’s Green.

We assume that S¢. Steplhen’s is dead and that “‘Hermes’’

has risen from its ashes. Besides various literary and
other articles, the journal contains University Cyollege
notes, and letters from the Queen’s College, Belfast,
and Queen’s College, Galway, and Queen’s College,
Cork. We find that in the course of the letter from
the last named a high tribute is paid to one of our
contributors, “Lee,”” of whom, no doubt, Cork men are
reasonably proud. We read:—“The essays of ‘Lee’
in the LEaDEr are written in a poetic prose which we
cannot consider inferior to that of Peaccck or George
Eliot.”” Mr. Aedan Cox writes on Mr. Yeat's poetry.
Canon O’Leary—3n T Ataip Peavaj—gives a first in-
stalment of Ceatlacan Capit. Mr. Maurice .
Healy writes “ A Short History of Irish Industry.’”
In the course of this article the writer, referring to the
Gaelic League and tho LEADER, says:—“In 1893 a
number of students gathered in a back room in Dublin
to found the Gaelic League. The movement received
but scanty encouragement ; it was too peaceful for the
Fel_ua.ns, a side issue for the Parliamentarians. It
retu‘ed. into obscurity to develop, and reappeared
mysteriously, as a strong and widespread organisation,
during the last days of the nineteenth century. A
weekly journal, not even officially connected with this
new body, began to spread the light in language which
was, to say the least of it, vigorous. —Under the
healthy influence of the League and its champion, the
few manufacturers remaining began to crawl out of
the mire, and to live again.”

The article concludes:— Under the protection of
such powerful friends the prospects of Ireland look
rosier than they have done for a long time. Guarded
by the Association with its ever watchful Secretary,
represented in Parliament by so zealous a friend as Mr.
Boland, stung and whipped into activity by a paper so
vigorously truthful as the LEeapEr our trades should
develop and blossom forth as of old; and may God
grant that no future disaster may blight the fair
growth.”” “Hermes’’ is excellently printed and
;urned out, andthe yearly subscription is 1s. 6d. post
Tee.

Mr. T. O’Reilly, Emperor of the Bungs in Ireland for
1907, presided at the complimentary dinner to Mr.
Thomas Gethings, ex-Emperor. Mr. O’Reilly proposed
the toast of “Our Native Land.”” We suppose Tre-
land a Nation ” would not be non-political enough for
the Bungs! Mr. O’Reilly said that “love of country
was one of our national characteristics.””  Ho might
have added that, fortunately for the tills of the Bungs,
love of drink is a characteristic of a great many of
Bung’s customers. %

Dr. Hillis, of Rathmines, is a very funny man.
Indeed, if he went on the ’alls he might make his for-
tune. e might appear on the bills of Dan Lowry’s as
“ Hillis, the side-splitting sawbones.”” The Rathmines
Urban Council received a letter from the Gaelic League,
and the Chairman, the innocent and bBland Mr.
Edmundson, stated that it was “ dated 24 Upper O’Con-
nell Street.”” By the way, we thought that dates had
to do with time, and not with places; however, we
bow to the superior knowledge of the learned French
scholar who presides over the rather exclusive Council
that have run up the rates to 10s. 6d. in the £. To
reburn to the funny man—Dr. Hillis, who evidently
mistook his vocation, and gave those abilities to medi-
cine that would have adorned the ’alls, asked “ Where
is that?’ And old Mr. Edmundson, not to be out-

~done in hilarity, remarked, “Somewhere in Dublin, I

_ believe,”” whereat the “Saved’’ Council broke into
rippling laughter. What gay old dogs they must be!
Perhaps they never heard of O’Connell; but, no doubt,
they know, all about Martin Luther and Henry VIII.

Monsignor Hallinan presided last Sunday at the
annual Eencml 1nec(;iugpof the St. Tta’s branch of the
Gaelic League, Newcastle, West.. The report stated
that the night classes had been in a more flourishing
condition than during any previous year. The num-
bers attending had been trebled. New branches of the
League have been started and are in full swing in the
parishes of Kilmeedy, Kileedy, Ashford and Monagea, L
which, together with those of Castlemahon, Feohenagh,
and Newcastle West, already in existence, form the
Coipoe Cean Taip of Newcastle West. The Irish,
too, is being successfully taught in the schools of those
parishes by Mr. Thomas Corbett, to whom is also due
the present satisfactory condition of the League
Branches in the district. Monsignor Hallinan, who, as
our readers know, has done great practical work for
the Irish language, paid a tribute to the teachers, Mr.
Foley and Mr. Corbett, and exhorted those who could
not be reasonably expected to learn the language them-
selves, to help on the movement in various ways, par-
ticularly by seeing that their children learn it.

What an offect a little bit of competition has upou
railways. Railways in Ireland are, to a considerable
extent, monopolies, and can snap their fingers at the
unfortunate and long suffering travelling public and
traders. Mr. James McGeoun, J.P., of Omagh and
Gortin, has a tale to tell which throws an instructive
light on our nation-kiilers. A letter which he wrote
and which was read at a meeting held at Greencastle,
is published in our contemporary, the “Ulster Herald.’
Mr. McGeoun purchased two wagons in Liverpool in
1902—at the moment we will not stop to.ask why he
did not buy Irish manufactured wagons. He had them
sent on to Belfast, but did not give instructions to
have them sent further. We give the rest of the tale
in Mr. McGeoun’s own words:—“ On arriving home, T
wrote the goods manager Great Northern Riailway, Bel-
fast, asking him to quote lowest carriage for two
to Omagh, and saying if their charge was not moderate
I would have them sent on by the Northern Counties
to Cookstown, as Cookstown was almost as near my
place here as Omagh. Meantime the Great Northern
Railway Company had lifted the wagons and brought
them on to Omagh, without any instructions, and for
which T received a bill for £7 9s. 4d. carriage on same.
I had no other alternative but to pay, which I did
that evening. A few days afterwards I received a
rgply from the goods manager G.N.R., saying the car-
riage would be £3 17s., of course then I had to get a
refund of £3 19., which was almost 50 per cent. below -
their regular rate, simply because there was a little bil
of competition in that direction. The following year I
got on two more and the very same thing happened. T
goes to show you the use a railway would be to thi
part of the country if it was once in operation. Tn the

have lost were it not for the Norbhorn Counties.”

FEBRUARY 16, 1907.

In the same paper, the ¢ Ulster’ Herald,”” wo read an
account of a memorial erected in Killyclogher Catholic
church. The marble for the monument was procured
from Kilkenny, and the report in our contemporary
states that “The Irish marble, strange to say, costs
more to bring to various parts of Ireland than the im-
ported article from Italy and Scotland.”

The production of “Rosaleen Dhu’’ at the Rotunda
last week was, we are glad to learn, a great success.
Large audiences witnessed the several performances,
and the acting was very creditable to the Drapers’
Dramatic Class. “Rosaleen Dhu’’ is an Anglo-Irish
drama of the good old-fashioned type, in the last act
of which virtue triumphantly prevails and vice is dis-
comfited. The performances were enlivened by the
introduction of various singing and dancing items,
which were much appreciated, particularly the duet
sung. by the actors who performed the parts of land-
lord and bailiff, concerning various topical matters.

If we are to take the promoters at their own word,
the. principal object of the International Exhibition is
the promotion of Irish industries. Well, as to its
final effect, we shall see what we shall see though we may
have to wait for some time, as the ultimate results of
big shows of this kind are not always felt immediately
after the gates are closed. The promoters will, how-
ever, during the progress of the Exhibition, have

pportunity to give some earnmest of their sym-

pathy with Irish manufacture. Take the refreshment
department, for instance. We hope that Irish-manu-
factured hop bitters and minerals, etc., will not be
found wanting in plenty there. There will, no doubt,
be a.big consumption in the tobacco line at the Exhi-
bition. The excellence of Irish-manufactured tobacco,
cigarettes and cigars is well known, and we trust that
the promoters will take the practical step of ensuring
that this important Irish industry will profit by the
Exhibition demand.

Under the heading “ The Dublin Leader,” we find
in the “ Southern Cross,”” Australia, a letter which our
contemporary describes as from “an esteemed corres-
pondent.”” Tt dea’s with Irish industrial matters and
the backwardness of Irish business men in advertising.
The letter concludes in this way:—“The LEADER is
read eagerly all over the world, and here in Australia
i3 looked up to with esteem that its clearsightedness
in detecting and dogged perseverance in fighting the
wrongs of Ireland that lie within reach deserves. When
will Irishmen in business become business men?  If
that is never to be—if some cannot be got to help them-
selves—could they not at least accept the help offered
from a quarter so well able to afford it?”

We find it necessary to repeat that communications
unaccompanied by names and addresses, and which con-
tain allegations, are at the best suspect. Probably
in most cases in which anonymous commuaications
reach us, the information they contain is correct, and
the communications are quite honest, but that is not
good enough for us. If correspondents refuse to trust
us with their names and addresses in confidence, how
can they expect us to trust in their reliability?

Beamish and Crawford, happily, did not succeed in
getting their proposed drinking club registered after
all. A few recent articles that appeared in the LEADER,
have placed our readers in possession of the main facts
concerning this attempt to add a new drink club to
Cork. The idea of attaching a drinking ciub to &
brewery was one that surely could only ferment in
the audacious brain of a Bung. Thirteen magistrates
turned up, so perhaps Beamish is inclined to be super-
stitious now! The Brewery party attempted to turn
Sir Edward Fitzgerald off the bench on the ground that
he had prejudged the case; but Sir Edward declined to
budge before the Bungites. =~ We suppose that the
Bungites would contend that all those who wquld
eventually vote for the new drinking cub came into
the Court with unbiased minds! The application was
refused.

We have received a rather long type-writien letter
from Miss Horniman, the British lady who endowed the
Abbey Theatre. As “To the Editor of "’ is typed and
“The Leader’’ is written in in ink, we assume that the
letter has been sent to numbers of journals, and that 1:t
will appear in Wednesday’s daily papers. We think it
is only necessary for us to refer to it. Miss Horniman
says that “when there was a threat to pull down the
curtains, it became their (the directors) duty to call
in the police.”” Certainly this is the first we ever heard
of any threat to pull down the curtains. She says:—
“ They knew that I have no prejudices on that subject,
because I approved of their action when the police were
called in to put a stop to low behaviour after a per-
formance last October.”” This, again, is the first we
heard of calling in the police in consequence of low
behaviour after a performance last October.”” Perhaps
Miss Horniman and the directors of the theatre would
give us particulars.

Whatever qualities Miss Horniman possesses, we
fear sho does not suffer from an over-dose of a sense
of humour. She writes:—“ An audience who calls
out ‘kill the author” is culous when it takes ex-
ception to imaginary culpable homicide. No jury
would bring in a verdict of wilful murder against
Christy Mahon under the circumstances.”” Might we
be permitted to express our view that the person ridi-
culous in this connection is Miss Horniman herself.
We never heard that “the audience’’ called out “kill
the author;’’ we believe it is stated that “a voice’”
cjaculated that bantering, and, under all the circum-
stances, we should say, rather witty remark. How-
ever, it was not as witty as the retort which “a voice’
on one of the nights let fly at one of the noisy cham-
pions of “the soul of the nation’’—the “voice’’
advised the champion of “freedom of judgment’ to
“go home and kill your father.”” This retort must
have escaped Miss Horniman, else her British and
lack-humour soul would surely have manifested her
righteous indignation at this piece of “wild Irish’
and blood-thirsty advice!

Miss Horniman says:—“The title of the Irish
National Theatre Society was settled long before I gave
my help. I would not subsidise either a Nationalist
or a Unionist theatre; it should be clearly understood

jammmmDUBUNﬁ

that I aided the Society solely from my desire to
assist thereby dramatic art. I intend to continue my
present arrangements at the Abbey Theatre until
Christmas, 1910, when the patent will lapse. * By that
time ‘either the theatre-going population of Dublin will
have given us such support that it will be worth while
for me to apply for a renewal of the patent, or else I
shall see that further efforts would be a waste of time
and money. A fair amount of encouragement would
soon improve the performances in every way, and a
great art centre might be made in Dublin which would
be the envy of other cities. Whether we shall succeed
or fail in the end rests in the hands of the general
public, not in the hands of any league or society how-
ever powerful.’’

The last sentence is a bit out of tune, we think, with
the traditional air of the directors. Miss Horniman’s
“rests in the hands of the general public,”’ does mnot
agree with the victualler-like take-it-or-leave-it attitude
of the actual runners of this “ art ’’ show. The latter
appears to wish to ram what they like down the throats
of the public, and when a piece like “ The Eloquent
Dempsey”” becomes popular on “a certain weakly poet,”
as we might style him if we wished, but whom our
skitty contributor of the Christmas number irreverently
referred to as “a chap called Yeats,”” inflicts one of
his or his friend’s poetic, ‘“long-felt wants’’ on those
who flock only to hear the popular play.

The plastering work of Humphrey’s Dump, a job
value for about £20,000 or so, was, we hear, given away
in London to a Scotch firm having a branch in Dublin.
No Irish firm, we understand, was asked to compete.
The Belfast contractors of Claremorris new church, we
understand, gave away the plastering to this Scotch
firm with the branch in Dublin, that is pulting the
plaster on Humphrey’s Dump, and the same plasterers
got the contract for the Diocesan College in Ballina.
‘We understand that the plastering contracts in the two
last mentioned cases were given away without com-
petition. By the way, recently we asked why the hall

in the Gresham  Hotel in which the Irish Industri
Conference social gathering was held, was called the
Aberdeen Hall. 'From what we'hear it is not without
veason that its name is Scotch. A Dublin firm got the
contract for building the hall, but we understand the -
roof was sub-let to one Scotch. firm, and the plastering
to another Scotch firm.  Under the circumstances,
Aberdeen was not an unappropriate name for the hall.

According to the King’s speech, we are to have pro-
posals with a view to “further associating the people of ~
Treland with the management of their domestic affairs,
and for otherwise improving the system of govern-
ment in its administrative and financial aspects,’” also,
an attempt with a view to “a reform of University
Education in Ireland, whereby I trust that the difficul-
ties which have so long retarded the development of
higher education in that country (Irelund)A may be
removed.” In view of this situation, would it not be®
an opportune time for Mr. O’Brien to throw in his lot
with those whose particular business it now becomes te
make the best of the turn affairs have taken, that is
if the proposals are such as can be reasonably con-

sidered.

A new series of post-cards, “St. Patrick’s Day
Caxrds,” has just been published by Messrs. Guy and
Co., Ltd., Cork. The series includes eight new designs
at 1d. each, and folding cards, with shamrock insets, at
3d. and 4d. each. Samples will be sent on applica-
tion.

Those of our readers who intend to send shamrock
to friends through the post will be interested in the
announcement of Messrs. P. O’Reilly, Poolbeg Street,
which appears in another column. The Tara series of
boxes which Messrs. O’Reilly have brought out as
usual this year are admirably suited for the purpose,
and can be had at 1d., 2d. and 3d. each from all
stationers and florists. A sample set, containing one
box of each size, will be forwarded post free on receipt
of 9d. in stamps.

Tue Royal Commission appointed to inquire into
the financial relations between Great Britain and

K QUESTION OF MONUMENTS.

EMORIAL monuments, celebrating various
M things and persons, are springing up in many
parts of Ireland. They are of various degrees of merit
and demerit, but mainly of demerit; some of them are
art, and some of them are not. Mr. Oliver Sheppard,
R.H.A., did a figure for a County Wexford memorial
a few years ago, and it proved a very fine thing. It
was for a ’98 memorial, and represented a peasant run-
ning with a pike in his hand, evidently about to join a
charge. It was as full of life, of the sense of motion,
as any figure I have seen, and we should all welcome
many things like it. The Sarsfield memorial at Lime-
rick is another thing which satisfies one, so full of life
and cnergy is the figure of the hero of Limerick.
Would that all our monuments were like these.

Now for the other kind of monuments. Dublin itself
can boast of a few of them; not so direly bad as the
howling things in some of the southern country towns,
but certainly bad enough. What an absurd monstro-
sity is the Moore statue! And is the Father Mathew
memorial so much better? That slim, grey ghost on its
thin perch of a pedestal always lowers my spirits. Still,
these things have something of the look of statuary
about them; bad as they may be, they are at least
better than, say, the ’98 memorial in the Market Square
of Thurles, or another monument which graces the town
of Clonmel. In these latter productions we see stunted
figures of peasant boys, not like anything in life. Look
ab the legs of the Thurles statue, and ask yourself what
life study, or anatomical study, can the man who
carved that figure ever have done? And what a mean-
looking pedestal the thing has, to boot! Again, look
at the Kickham statue in Tipperary town. True, it is
better than the other two figures, but how poor it is,
after all. Then the upper part of the pedestal juts out
in an ambitious ledge, which practically hides the
undersized figure above from any person standing near
the monument. As for the city of Cork, it now pos-
sesses a National Memorial, not commemorative of any-
thing in particular, but of nationality—or national feel-
ing—in a vague, general way. This “work of Goths
and Vandals ”’ I have not yet seen; happily it was not
vet erected when I was in Cork. I have, however,
heard the private groans of some friends who have seen
it, and who lament it bitterly, as a patent exhibition of
bad art, or rather no art, and a shameful waste of
money. 5

Now, as Mr. Dooley says,

“T see be the pa-apers”
that there is in eourse of erection in Tipperary town

Gl

another National monument, this time to the Manches-
ter Martyrs. Indeed, it is to be unveiled so soon as
March 10th. A picture of the design was lately given
in one of the evening papers, and though it seems to
promise being an improvement on the things at Thurles
and Clonmel, yet that is about the best that can be
said of it.  According to the printed design it looks
just like a glorified cemetery monument. The name
of the designer is given, and it is not one that I have
yet heard of in the world of art. The gentleman is
described as “C.E.,” which may wairant him as fit
to lay roads or devise drainage plans, but which in itself
ha,r‘_dly proves him quite the best man for designing
artistic monuments. The sculptor employed is described
as “ that well-known sculptor, , of Dublin,”
but I don’t remember having ever heard of him, so that
he can hardly be so very well-known as the press notice
L_’leclz\res. Judging the monument by the printed design,
it looks one of those things better left unerected.

If we are to have monuments at all, why not good
ones? The erection of. these things shows a low stan-
dard of taste, or rather, no standard at all of taste, on
the part of those who are satisfied with such designs. I
suppose the saying of this will give offence, perhaps
pain, but that is certainly not my intention. = We have
unhappily got into such a sore, querulous, susceptible
state of mind in this country, that the catalogue of
things one must not discuss, or criticise, or even think
about, scems to be rapidly growing. We have a morbid
desire to shut out “ foreign’’ literary influences, lest
the Irishness of Ireland might be contaminated by new
models from other lands. We are to build up Ireland
exclusively from within, with purely native materials
and native models. I do not care to discuss the pos-
sible success of this in letters, but in the art of sculp-
ture I can only say it will not do. Sinn Fein in art will
hardly thrive, if carried out upon lines of pure exclu-
sion. We may use Irish subjects, and types of feature
recognisably Irish, but the technique of art must be the
same as that in vogue elsewhere—if we are to do any
good in statuary, at all events. Where did Foley learn?
Where did Hogan study? Not, certainly, in a monu-
mental sculptor’s yard.  Yet the things in Clonmel
and Thurles bespeak no better training, so far as I
could judge. This idea of ours that we, who lack the
technique of any modern art, or ancient one, for that
matter, can afford to ignore technique, and go forward
upon some kind of Sinn Feinism in art—hoping, no
doubt, eventually to evolve a technique of our own—
would be ludicrous were. its results not so painful to
behold. Then the notion that the execution of any
memorial work must be given to a “local’” man
bespeaks a frame of mind that is as parochial in art
as it is in patriotism. 3

I am in no way despising the well-intended efforts of
those who are labouring to commemorate events of stir-
ring memory, or men who made sacrifices for Ireland—
or for the National movement of their own day, which
is perhaps the same thing. But I cannot contemplate
with satisfaction the uprise of monuments like those I
have animadverted upon. It were surely better to get
proper statues, done by real artists, like Mr. Sheppard
or Mr. John Hughes, or else to have no statues at all.
If patriotic localities needs must have memorial monu-
ments, let them, if they cannot afford to employ a real,
high-class artistic sculptor, content themselves with the
erection of a memorial like that at Bandon. The ele-
gant column erected there is an ornament to the town,
and its presence gracefully arrests the eye the moment
wo get into its neighbourhood. It is nothing wonderful,
no doubt, but it is decent and sufficiently artistic, and
much preferable to the Crampton monument in Bruns-
wick St., Dublin, or even the new Burgh Quay memo-
rial, though the latter is surely better than the statue
in the Market Square of Thurles! If our people want
art they will have to pay for art; if they cannot afford
that, let them be content to go without it.  Taaaw.

THE LEADER.

FEBRUARY 16, 1907.

MR. GWYNN, M.P., AND THE SCHOOL
MANAGERS.

N the series of articles which I have written on the
«Jacques’’ pamphlet, I have pointed out that

whilst the author, in his efforts to stand on many stools
at the same time, and to reconcile things that, by their
nature, were irreconcilable, fell into many inconsis-
tencies and contradictions, yet was coherent and con-
sistent enough in one position—and that was, laying
down principles and proposals which, whatever elso they
may effect, would surely result in their logical working
out to the ending of the present managerial system
With specious words he tries to conceal his design, -
but the disguise is too thin, and can deceive nobody
who reads through the work carefully. I have pointed
out that his proposals are in direct opposition to the
solemn and repeated pronouncements of the Catholic
Hicrarchy of Ireland. The people of Ireland, in‘whose
interests he takes it on himself to speak, are not asking
for the change he advocates; and, furthermore, his pro-
posals would involve a further financial burden and in-
justice on the already over-taxed and unjustly-treated
people of this poor country. In my last article T stated
that, notwithstanding ~ all  these  considerations,
« Jacques’ *’ main principles are being now propagated
by different agencies through the country. I have
already dealt with one of these agencies.

I now propose to deal with what I consider the second
agency, and that is the Irish Parliamentary Party; so
far as it is represented by the speeches of some of its
most prominent members. I speak with some reserve,
and must define my position. In the first place, I do
not profess to be certain that the few members, whose
words and acts I intend to eriticise, do really speak in
the name of the whole Irish Party, or are merely ven-
tilating their own individual opinion. But I have not
scen any, repudiation by the Party as a whole, or any
of its members, of their language.

Again, I have not the honour of being personally
acquainted with the gentlemen whose words and acts
form the subject of my criticisms. My criticisms will
be directed exclusively to their public utterances on the
Primary Education Question, and in particular to their
bearing on the existing managerial system.

Furthermore, whilst I recognise the distinction
between nationality and politics, and believe that in the
past the politicians unwarrantably claimed too large a
share, if not a monopoly, of patriotism, yet I am a
Dbeliever in the need of a Parliamentary Party to win
our rights from an alien legislature, and hence am,
though not an active politician, a supporter of the pre-
sent Parliamentary Party. But whilst this is true, L
do not mean that I approve of every line of policy it
pursues, nor do I feel myself debarred from criticising
the public acts or speeches of its members. T do it,
however, not with any wish or intention to impair in ~
the least the unity and solidarity of the Party,
but rather with a view to point out certain dangers
ahead, which it may be well to discuss and take stock of.
Having said so much to clear the air, let me take the
words and acts of the latest accession to the ranks of the
Party, Mr. Stephen Gwynn, member for Galway. I
take him first, not because I believe him to be the most
important or responsible member of the Party, but
because he makes himself such. At a meeting held ab
Wicklow recently to discuss educational matters, he said
he was there in a dual capacity, both as representative
of the Gaelic League and of the Parliamentary Party.
He thus arrogates to himself a position of singular im-
portance before the nation. In fact, in a broad sense,
he takes on himself to speak for all Ireland; because the
Ireland outside th eranks of the Parliamentary Party
and its sympathisers, and the Gaelic League and its
followers and sympathisers, is comparatively insignifi-
cant, so far as members go, at least.  As in my criticism .
of the “Jacques” pamphlet I had to grope my way
through its pages to discover the credentials of the
author, and his fitness for the work he took on hands;
so in the present case, in the absence of personal

acquaintance with Mr. Gwynn, I must repeat the same
Progcess. -

He has written a book called “The Fair Hills

of Ireland.” It is substantially a guide book, for some
of tho most interesting and historic places in Ireland,
containing a good deal of interesting :information,
written in an attractive style, and in a volume which,
for letter-press and illustration, is a credit to the pub-
lishers. At page 120, alluding to the Cathedral of
Armagh, we read the following : —“ Outside the western
doorway stand two statues, and onme of these is the
late Archbishop M‘Gettigan, whose beautiful profile, as
I saw it outlined against an evening sky, redeemed the
meanness of the sculpture. I remember a near kins-
woman of his, born on the same mountain side above a
wild lake in Donegal, who held her head high by reason
of her kinship with the prelate, though she was
& servant  in  my father’s house.” In a
similar strain he writes of a living dignitary of the
Catholic Church, whose name is a household word
through the length and breadth of the land, and who s
revercd and loved by Irish Catholics all the world over.
Now I have no reason to doubt the accuracy of the
writer’s statement, but I do question his sense of pro-
priety and good taste. This style of writing jars on one’s
feelings—makes people think that Mr. Gwynn is alto-
gether a very superiar kind of person, knows that he is,
and would have the whole world know it, too. It is
quite possible that there is a servant in the house of
some Catholic prelate who holds her head high because
she is a near relation of Mr. Gwynn, M.P.; but if that
prelate were writing a book, and were quite needlessly
to introduce that fact into it, would not Mr. Gwynn be
disposed to consider it an impertinence? A little fur-
ther on, still speaking of the same two prelates, he
says:—* But what chance had these men of general cul-
ture? There was no University where education could
be accepted, at least, by any Catholic designed for the
priesthood ; but the state, so rigorous in this refusal,
thoughtfully provided a theological seminary where they
would be drilled and disciplined in the learning of a
Roman priest. Everything was done that could be done
to make a Roman ecclesiastic; how little care was taken
to make a cultivated Irishman.”” If the ethics of cul-
zure consist in certain external embellishments, assump-
#ions of accents, and manners, airs of condescension, and
various other counterfeits, the word in that sense may be
applicable to others, but not to these two dignitaries;
but if culture consist in close resemblance to our Blessed
Lotrd, Who was, Mr. Gwynn will, T suppose, admit, the
truest and noblest type of Christian culture, then I
say, and I speak from personal knowledge, the deep
humility, the unassuming manners, combined with the
high station of these two Catholic churchmen, entitle
them, in the best sense of the words, to be called “ cul-
tivated Ivishmen.”” but this is “en passant.”

From a gentleman who writes in this strain, one is pre-
pared for something beyond the ordinary. As a speech-
maker and legislator, whoever reads his speeches on edu-
cational matters will not be disappointed. Let me take
as an example, his speech delivered at a meeting held in
Galway on the 24th of last November. The meeting was
one of a series which have been held in different parts
of the country during the last five or six months to pro-
dest against the starvation policy of the Government re-
garding the primary cducation of the country. This
‘policy of starvation has.been pursued to the detriment
.of the teacheérs’ position by inadequate salaries, slow
‘promotion, atbempts at amalgamation of schools, miser-
:able retiring pensions, all of which naturally generate a
spirit of dissatisfaction and unrest amongst the teachers.
They have been also convened to protest against the
great financial robbery of Irish education by the British
Treasury for several years, the most glaring example
of which is the misapplication of the Equivalent Grant
to other purposes than that to which it rightly belongs.
‘Now, with all these objects. the whole coun-h‘y-—clergy
and laity, managers and teachers, and most public
bodies—heartily sympathise. ~The resolutions framed
for, and passed at, these meetings, as a rule, dealt with
and covered these grievances only. But unfortunately

some spealkers have been using these occasions to ventilate
their views on other matters which the meetings: were
not called to discuss, and which are of a highly conten-
tious character. One of the worst offenders in this re-
spect is, to my mind, Mr. Gwynn. The Venerable
Bishop of Galway, the Most Rev. Dr. MacCormack,
presided at the Galway meeting, and in his opening
speech according to the “Freeman’s Journal’
report, he very truly said:—“If the Com-
missioners of National Education would only
condescend to take the opinions of the managers.
and teachers of the country, he thought these changes
would be more judicious. No body of men in Ireland
understood the working of their own schools as well as
the managers and teachers.” These weighty observa-
tions, coming from such a source, one would imagine,
ought be sufficient to check the zeal of the most ardent
opponent of the present managerial system. But the
young Parliamentary hand was not to be restrained.
He seems like one who thinks he has got a commission
to discharge—perhaps he has—and that is, to be pok-
ing at the managerial question. In his speech
he alludes rather patronisingly to a Catholic priest, and
then takes to booming a Protestant prelate as follows—
He did not think that the “case against the Treasury
and againsb the British Government had been so strongly
stated by any man in Ireland as by Dr. Peacock, the
Protestant Archbishop of Dublin.””  If Mr. Gwynn
had been better informed, he would have known that
the case against the Treasury and the British Govern-
ment had been stated as strongly and ably by others—
Catholic priests and lay people—as.by Dr. Peacock, and
long before the latter spoke on the subject; and that
what was said by him was only a summary or rehash
of what was stated by others. But perhaps in the eyes
of Mr. Gwynn it made all the difference in the world
that a Protestant prelate should condescend to sympa-
thise with the teachers, or have the courage to protest
against the doings of the British Treasury and Govern-
ment, and that fact had to be emphasised.

I am speaking of the impression the reading of his
speech has made on my own mind. I should be very
glad to think that I have been mistaken in the view
I take of it. Further on he is thus reported:—“ He
believed the thing most urgently needed of all the dif-
ferent forms of Home Rule, which he believed to be
absolutely imperative—the most urgently needed was,
a national body to control the finance and direction of
education, a body which should be representative of the
nation and responsible to the nation.”” What is the
meaning of this language in the mouth of a Home
Ruler? Is Mr. Gwynn looking for Home Rule? Then
if he is, let him try to get it, and when we have got
that, it will be time for the Irish nation to make its
own laws, and solve its own educational problems. Is
it not trifling with our intelligence and with our national
interests to have persons, calling themselves Home
Rulers, professing on public platforms that nothing
less than the power to make their own laws
will satisfy them, whittling down their claims at the
same time, and placing on the forefront of their demands
from the English Government another Board “ to con-
trol the finance and direction of education?’ TIs there
not such a Board already? -Why not press the Govern-
ment to change the  personnel”’ of that Board, and
place on it representatives of the educational interests
of the country—the managers, teachers, and others cap-
able of directing education? What does Mr. Gwynn, and
others who are using it, mean by the new platform
shibboleth “ respresentative of the nation and respon-
sible to the nation?’ Would not a certain number of
representatives of the managers, and of the teachers, and
of other educational interests—on a Board, independent
of the English Government, like the existing Board of
National Education, but honestly financed, and not
robbed as at present by the British Treasury—be as
“representative of the nation and responsible to the
nation,”” and more suitable to control educational
matters than the nominees of persons who know little o1
nothing of things educational? Would not such a Board
be as “representative of the nation, and as responsible

RECENT writer in “.an Clarveam Souup,”

sation at large, we need not feel uneasy as to the just

eight or nine years.

He has recently added to his enormities by publish-
ing “faoipcin Naom-Péopais.”  We have here the
original Latin of St. Patrick’s “ Confessio,” with an
Irish translation, by an c-Adcan Paopars, and an
English version for the use of.the students. The
wonder is, that this document, of such interest to the
TIrish people, should have waited for more than fourteen
centuries before being put into the language of the
country.

The “ Confessio’’ has many interests. It is one of
the historical records of our country, telling of the com-
ing of that religion with which Ireland has been so
closely associated down to the present day. It is,
besides that, the revealing of a soul—of a great soul
called to a great mission. Heretofore Patrick has been
as little of a reality to us as any of the great heroes of
antiquity; he was more shadowy than Fionn or
Cuchullin. He has been known to us chiefly as a
worker of almost incredible wonders, beside some of
which the exploits of the heroes were tales of every
day. The “Confessio’’ shows us Patrick, the man
who, in his youth, “believed not in the living God,”
who was many times tempted, who was opposed by some
of his seniors, and who sometimes displeased them and
others rather than do violence to his conscience It
is a very human Patrick that tells us this story, and
the human in us is stirred responsive to the human
crying from him.

Here we: have also Patrick the Apostle, the man of
great and simple faith, who heard the call, and braved
all dangers to obey it, whose prayer was “ that He
should grant me to drink of His chalice, as He hath
The very lan-

guage of the tale almost compels our belief. It is not

 the language of the visionary or the fanatic. It is the

languags of faith. Tt is almost the language of
inspiration.

To Patrick the Scripture must have been an open
book, a familiar study. The  Confessio’’ is impreg-
nated with its language. Not only does he quote freely
from the sacred books, but scriptural phrases and terms
of expression abound throughout the narrative. The
editor devotes ten pages of notes to illustrating this
featuro of the story. He has also some interesting
notes as to MSS., variants, etc.

It is unnecessary to speak of the literary merits of
Father Dinneen’s version. He has evidently endea-
voured to reproduce the spirit of the Latin in his trans-
lation, which reminds us much more of the original than
of any English version.

The book has been printed by Browne and Nolan, and
published by Gill, and is to be had in_three styles of
binding, and at such a little cost as makes it inexcus-
able for anyoue to be without a copy.

INTERMEDIATE RULES—A REPLY.

b ATHOLIC Headmaster”’ is entitled to the

thanks of your readers for drawing their atten-
tion to the new Intermediate Progtamme, which as-
suredly demands the most careful consideration.  The
merits of the Programme are, of course, to some extent,
a matter forexperts; but any educated person is capable
of forming a correct judgment on the broad issues in-
volved, some of which are definitely raised by “ Catholic
Headmaster ”’ in his interesting letters. As the wel-
fare of the country is so intimately connected with the
character of the education imparted in our Secondary
Schools, it is of the utmost importance that as much
light as possible should be thrown on the radical changes
which the new Programme introduces into the so-called
Intermediate System. Ilence I am sure I need not
apologize to you or to “ Catholic Headmaster ’’ for ven-
turing to lay before your readers my reasons for dis-
senting emphatically from the approbation which
“ Catholic Headmaster ’ bestows on those changes.

From his letter I gather that his reply to the cir-
cular addressed by the Board to the heads of Interme-
diate Schools was that he considered it *“ undesirable to
make English and two other languages obligatory on all
candidates, because the number of obligatory subjects
ought not to be increased ;’’ but now he assures us that
“he has no objection to the rule requiring two lan-
guages (with English)!”” This inconsistency and his
incredible assertion that practically every boy can be
got to pass if the school has a sufficiently large staff of
competent teachers, detract very much from the weight
one might be inclined to attach to his letter. However,
let us examine the points urged by him in favour of
the new Programme.

“The two-language Rule will,”” he says,  present
no difficulty whatever to most schools.”  Yet the
Headmaster of one of the most successful schools in
the country, a past President of his Association, stated
quite recently that this Rule would have caused the
failure of more than half the boys that passed at the
last examination from his school; and his staff is
certainly large enough and most competent, all being
graduates of standing and experience. Other Head-
masters of prominent schools have expressed unquali-
fied disapproval of this latest effort to attach undue im-
portance to literary studies, and agreed that very

serious difficulty and even grave injustice would
result.  Unquestionably, in consequence of “the
two-language Rule,”” the course in Arithmetic
has been completely spoiled, and many can-

didates will be hindered from taking up Book-keeping,
Shorthand, History and Geography, or a second Science
subject, though for them these subjects may be far more
important than a smattering of French. “ Catholic
Headmaster *’ tells us, also, that ¢ 97 per cent. of the
pupils of his school passed in two languages.”” As it
stands, this statement has not the slightest value. He
does not say what proportion passed the examination
generally, nor what other subjects they presented, nor
in how many of these they were successful. So we do
not know whether they passed at all; nor have we the
information necessary to judge how they would have
fared had they been affected by the new Rules; nor can
we tell whether his school is a fair type. In some
schools, owing to diocesan regulations, scholarships, and
the exercise of considerable pressure, the number of
talented boys is far above the average. 3

4.4

He speaks of “the raising of Arithmetic from being
@ mere pass subject ’’ as “ one of the chief features of
the new Programme.’” It has certainly been raised
wonderfully. Tt is merged with, or rather submerged
in Algebra. The two together are treated as one sec-
tion of Mathematics, and will appear on the same
paper. One-third of the pass paper and one-tenth of
the honours paper will be devoted to Arithmetic! As
a pass may be easily obtained on the Algebra alone,
therq will be henceforth little or no inducement for any
candidate in any Grade to trouble himself much about
A-rlt»h.mcbic; while every candidate in every Grade must
Pass in two modern languages with English! So is
A.nthmetic marvellously exalted as becomes a subject of
vnbz\l'imporbance to thousands of Intermediate students.
“Raised,” indeed! Wiy, ‘it has practically dis-
appeared from the Programme!

Sppposo a pupil does turn his attention to Arith-
wetic, what course is prescribed? In Junior Grade,
Practice and Interest are supposed to be enough for the
year or two spent in that Grade; Percentages, Aver-
ages, and Square Root are considered sufficient for
another year or two in Middle Grade; and, in their
Senior Grade year, students are taken quietly through
P_roﬁt and Loss, Discount, and Stocks! Let us con-
sider for a moment that boys leave the Junior Grade
usually in their teenth or seventeenth year; the
Middle Grade, in their seventeenth or eighteenth year,
and we shall see what a miserable preparation for the
merchant’s office is in store for the victims of the new
Programme: Even in the Senior Grade pass course in
Arithmetic and Algebra, no mention is now made of
Progressions, of Annuities, of Logarithms; and the only
scrap of Mensuration preserved, rectangular areas and
volumes, appears in Junior Grade Arithmetic and
Algebra. Thus the most important rules in Arithmetic,
and practically every rule in Mensuration, are either
blotted out of the Programme, or, appearing in Senior
Grade only, will be reached during their school days
by less than nine-tenths of those who are preparing for
business! Couple this treatment of Arithmetic with the
fact already alluded to, that very few commercial stu-
dents.will be able to add Book-keeping, or Shorthand,
or History and Geography to the number of subjects
new obligatory, and we are forced to conclude that the
interests of an important section of the community have
been either ignored or sacrificed for a very s'ight coat-
ing of French polish.

the extinction of those Intermediate Schools in country
towns, on which large districts depend as the only

LITERATURE AND IRELAND.

7/ OUR contributor, ‘“ Imaal,”’ in the issue of the
19th ult. of your paper, has written an article
on the origin, use and abuse of fiction. In it he com-
ments on some statements of mine in the previous
issue on the question of literature, which wers made in
connection with the discussion on Free Libraries. In
that article I stated my reasons for advocating the
general introduction of Irish literature among the
people, so it will not be necessary to restate them here.
“Imaal’s’’ analysis of fiction and of literature is good
as far as it goes, but he seems to have forgotten Ireland
altogether. The Greeks, Romans, Italians, English—
all are brought under contribution, but he has nothing
to say of the literature and civilisation of the Gael.
And this, be it remembered, in the days of the Irish-
Ireland movement, when the literati of ancient Giye
are expected, if ever, to have their champions.

I plead, then, for Irish literature, fiction and art,
first of all because they are Irish; secondly, on their
merits; thirdly, because they are essential if the mind
of Ireland is to be preserved; and fourthly, because by
supplying the native article we shall be stemming, in
the only effective way, the inroads of the British mind,
which is the surest form of conquest. Our forefathers
left us a valuable literary heritage, in quantity and
quality, and especially in the domain of imaginative -
literature. The long course of Irish history produced
a literature and a social life insular, strong and
national. ~Ireland then stood upon her own legs, and
evolved the various elements of her polity from her own
thought and consciousness. But, at the same time, she
was not so blind nor so bigoted as not to employ what-
ever was best in classical literature and art for the per-
fection of her own. She knew how to graft the foreign
branch on the Irish stem. The monks of the Middle
Ages studied Greeck and Latin authors without preju-
dice to the culture of Ireland. This was the system
that produced Dun Scotus and John Scotus Erigena in
philosophy, and gave us the beautiful drawings of the
Book of Kells. Let us, in our age, try and follow their
example.  The materials are about us ready for use.
Let us digest, modernise, reconstruct, create, and when
we have put our own house in order we can attend to
foreign master-pieces. I would prefer to hear less of
Homer and more of the Tin bo Cusitgne—at least
for some time to come. It is not such a great piece of
literature, perhaps, but it is Irish.

T repeat that literature is a history of man as he is
found, and not of man as he ought to be. It must be
a history of the whole man, his failings as well as his
virtues, otherwise the picture would be incomplete.
Newman says, “Idea of a University,”’ page 311: “It

THE LIBRARY QUESTION.

I HAVE followed with much interest the correspon-
. dence in your columns on the question of Free
Libraries versus Parochial Libraries. No doubt thero
is much to be said on both sides, but I sincerely hope
that our priests and bishops will not be led into any-
thing that savours of opposition to the Free Library
movement. No doubt Free Libraries are open -to
ab'use‘ if not properly safeguarded, but so are all in-
stitutions, and it would be a thousand pities if any-
thing were done to retard the advance of the move-
ment. Personally, T am not much concerned with the
question of whether Free or Parochial Libraries
should be established, but I am deeply coucerned that
the opportunities afforded to our people of reading
good books, even despised fiction, should be multiplied,
especially in rural “districts. There is nothing so
appalling in Ireland as the utter lifelessness of country
villages, especially in the winter months. Under exist-
ing conditions how many fine young fellows fall a
victim to the temptations of your friend, M, Bung,
_owing to the want of anything to fill up their time.
I am myself interested in a village in the Co. Lime-
rick. Tt is a progressive, go-ahead little place of
some 200 or 400 inhabitants, but unfortunately it has
about a dozen public-houses.  If sports or races are
got up the publicans give prizes on the understanding
that every inducement is offered to the young men to
frequent the public-houses. During the long dreary
winter there is no other house in the village where
the young men can resort, and be sure of a welcome.
What is the result? Year by year, when I go home
on my holidays, and inquire after my old school fellows
who have remained at home, I am told of some fresh
recruit to the drunkards’ ranks. A short time ago an
energetic curate hired a room to serve as a reading-
room. He was cold-shouldered by the publicans, who
mould the public opinion of the parish, and the
generous experiment failed, and the curate was shortly
afterwards transferred. A little later the whole of
the shopkeepers combined with a view of building a
